_notes: Migrated content from the No MOU Nashville campaign archive and {Rich Text} Substack. Upload this through the admin bar's + Import button in Merge mode (safe to re-run). Every article links to its primary source — nothing is re-hosted. Dates are taken from search results where available and are approximate where noted; edit them in the UI if you want exact values.

articles.title: Nashville's Unauthorized Secret Police Force And The People & Orgs Enabling It
articles.summary: The Nashville Downtown Partnership's budget has not been reviewed or approved by Metro Council in 22 years. Its president has doubled his salary past $500K while public money funds a private policing operation licensed through a shell company.

articles.title: Downtown Partnership's Specially Assessed Nonsense
articles.summary: Since 1998 the CBID has funneled obligatory special assessments through the DMC to the Downtown Partnership. The last Council-approved CBID budget was FY 2003-2004 — more than sixty million dollars have moved without the legally required Metro Council approval.

articles.title: Sunday Rewind: Freddie O'Connell — 'Strong Communities are Better for Safety than a Surveillance State' and Other Things He Reversed Course On
articles.summary: A look back at Mayor O'Connell's own record: voting to ban LPRs in 2017, warning in 2021 that strong communities beat a surveillance state, then reversing on Fusus, LPRs, and the $15M MOU once in the mayor's office.

articles.title: From the Makers of Fusus (Literally): Meet 'LeoSight' — Mayor's MOU Resurrects Rejected Surveillance Tools Under New Branding
articles.summary: LeoSight's CEO spent three years at Fusus through the Axon acquisition and took over LeoSight after Nashville rejected Fusus in April 2025. LeoSight itself is a rebrand of a 2015 company called Live Earth. The Mayor's MOU quietly reinstates the technology Nashville already said no to.

articles.title: Breaking: The Mayor's Official Coordinating Nashville's Surveillance MOU Helped Build ICE's Data Pipeline at LexisNexis
articles.summary: Kristin Wilson, Mayor O'Connell's Chief of Operations and Performance, is coordinating the $15M surveillance MOU between MNPD and NDP. Before public service she spent six years at LexisNexis Risk Solutions, which currently holds a $22.1M contract with ICE.

articles.title: State Records Reveal Nashville Downtown Partnership Was Never Getting the $15M
articles.summary: State documents show the District Management Corporation — not NDP — was awarded the grant, contradicting repeated claims by the Mayor's Office that the $15M was going directly to the Downtown Partnership. VERIFY URL: article exists but slug not confirmed from search index; update the link in-app when you find it.

articles.title: Breaking: Same Exec Who Literally Signed Fusus Contracts With Chief Drake Now Has New Surveillance Company in Mayor's MOU
articles.summary: The original MNPD-Fusus contract carried Mark Wood's signature alongside Chief Drake's. Now Wood runs LeoSight, and the Mayor's Thanksgiving-eve MOU routes $15M toward the same expanded capabilities, sidestepping procurement and public hearing requirements — a replay of the Fusus pattern.

articles.title: (Surveillance MOU) There Is No Use Amending the MOU Resolution — CMs' Attempts To Fix It Are Making It Worse
articles.summary: Metro Council already has 100% oversight over NDMC funds through Metro Ordinance BL2017-580 and 0% oversight over NDP funds. The MOU trades statutory line-item control for a voluntary agreement the mayor can amend without Council approval. Seven independent grounds for rejection — pick any one.

articles.title: Dispatch from the December 15th Budget and Finance Committee Meeting
articles.summary: On-the-ground reporting from the Budget and Finance Committee meeting where council members began dismantling the Mayor's claims that the MOU was needed to restrict what NDP could buy.

articles.title: The People's House: How Nashville Metro Council Is Starting To Reclaim Democratic Oversight from an Autocratic Mayor's Office
articles.summary: Plain text, council testimony, and a public records release show it was MNPD in concert with the Mayor's Office — not NDP — that put the most problematic surveillance technology in the MOU to begin with. The narrative that the MOU 'limited' what NDP could buy collapses on contact with the record.

articles.title: Correction About THP Trooper Throwing Man to Ground for Kicking His Car (After Almost Running Him Over)
articles.summary: Correction and context on the September 20, 2025 incident: THP Sgt. DeWunya Topps — off-duty in uniform for Solaren Risk Management — slammed a pedestrian to the ground after nearly running him over in a crosswalk. The man kicked one car, not 'cars' as earlier reported.

articles.title: In Wake of ICE Killing, O'Connell Files $3.38M Surveillance & Policing Donations via Funding Used for ICE Collaboration
articles.summary: After a fatal ICE encounter in Nashville, the Mayor's Office moved a $3.38M surveillance and policing donation package through funding streams that have previously flowed into ICE collaboration channels. Approximate date — verify in-app.

articles.title: Buckle Up: Another Fight Over Surveillance is Upon Us
articles.summary: Flock Safety just raised $275M at a $7.5B valuation led by Andreessen Horowitz. Nashville is the case study for how procurement is circumvented to eliminate public consent over surveillance — and LPRs feed a larger AI-driven infrastructure that can flow into Palantir. MNPD's budget still includes ~$2.8M for LPRs.

articles.title: We Built the Surveillance App Nashville's Leaders Have Been Asking For. Except It Tracks Them.
articles.summary: MNPD and the Mayor's Office keep pushing LPRs even as O'Connell and Chief Drake tout a 20% drop in crime, 18% drop in violent crime, and burglary at its lowest level since the 1960s — a contradiction the city's official messaging quietly papers over.

articles.title: A Nashville Proposal Could Outsource Surveillance and Policing to a Nonprofit
articles.summary: Truthout feature by Mike Lacy and Ed Vogel on how Nashville is leveraging a local nonprofit to build a public-private panopticon. Deep-dive analysis of how the Downtown Partnership MOU bypasses democratic oversight and revives the Fusus playbook under the LeoSight brand.

articles.title: Mayor's Office Files Resolution to Allow Surveillance Use
articles.summary: Nashville Scene's Pith in the Wind reports that the Mayor's Office quietly filed the MOU before Thanksgiving. The package included security cameras, noise-camera enforcement, a live video wall, and two separate real-time surveillance platforms — a backdoor route to the same tools rejected in the Fusus debate.

articles.title: Metro Council to Consider Downtown Partnership Security Grant
articles.summary: Nashville Banner's pre-hearing report on the $15M MOU, connecting LeoSight's leadership back to the Fusus contracts Mark Wood personally signed. Council Member Erin Evans tells the Banner she expects many members to seek a deferral.

articles.title: Safety or Surveillance? $15M Downtown Nashville Plan Sparks Debate
articles.summary: NewsChannel 5 on the widening split between city leadership and residents over whether a $15M state grant should fund downtown surveillance technology targeting vulnerable communities.

articles.title: Downtown Nashville Could See New Surveillance Tech After $15M State Grant
articles.summary: WKRN's roundup of the surveillance tools the MOU would enable: noise-detection cameras, AI-driven video analysis, and expansion of the existing downtown camera network via the Nashville Downtown Partnership.

articles.title: Nashville Surveillance Cameras Spark More Pushback as Metro Council Considers Expanded Technology
articles.summary: WPLN covers the community hearings as the $15M grant — already earmarked in part for new MNPD cameras — draws sustained opposition from residents, civil liberties groups, and music-community members.

articles.title: Nashville Council Rejects Downtown Surveillance Cameras in Split Vote on Safety Plan
articles.summary: Metro Council rejects 20-15 (with three abstentions) a $150,000 measure that would have added 15 new police surveillance cameras downtown — the first piece of the broader $15M plan to come to a vote after prior amendments stripped out LeoSight and Fivecast.

articles.title: TN-07 Results! Plus, Is Downtown Getting Mass Surveillance?
articles.summary: City Cast Nashville podcast: Steven Hale (Nashville Banner) joins host Marie Cecile Anderson and executive producer Whitney Pastorek to break down the $15M MOU, the armored SWAT vehicle, and the backlash.

articles.title: Increased Surveillance and Policing Does Not Make Nashville Safer
articles.summary: Open Table Nashville's statement of opposition to the MOU, arguing that security built on surveillance disproportionately targets refugees, asylum seekers, immigrants, LGBTQIA+ folks, and people of color — and doesn't make anyone safer.

articles.title: Nashville Residents Push Back as City Revives Police Surveillance Plan
articles.summary: Liberation News on the resident-led organizing that forced the deferrals and amendments, ultimately driving the camera proposal to a losing vote.

ctas.title: Read the primary-source reporting
ctas.description: {Rich Text} is the independent investigative newsletter that broke most of the surveillance, NDP, and MOU stories linked below. Subscribe, share, and support the reporting.

ctas.title: Demand Metro Council oversight of the Downtown Partnership
ctas.description: NDP's budget has not been reviewed by Metro Council in 22 years, even as it funnels specially-assessed public money into a private policing operation. Contact your council member and ask why.

ctas.title: Watch the MNPD surveillance budget
ctas.description: Even after the $15M MOU was stripped of LeoSight, Fivecast, and new cameras, MNPD's ongoing budget still carries roughly $2.8M for License Plate Recognition. Track every line item before the next Flock contract.

ctas.title: No MOU Nashville — campaign archive
ctas.description: Timeline, talking points, and the organizing record of how Nashville pushed back on the surveillance MOU and forced the council to reject new cameras in January 2026.
